evidence_quotes.quote: Regarding harmful advertising practices, I do believe that while influencers must be made responsible for knowingly endorsing harmful products, companies should also be included within the scope of this act.

Something for personalization that should be treated within this act is the lack of preemptive protection against known bad actors within those digital storefronts, all the while those still benefit from those actors' harmful actions through things like sales or sponsorships.

paragraphs.text: Hello. I am someone who plays games and uses many kinds of digital storefronts for shopping for both digital software such as games and creative apps, but also physical items through digital storefronts. I want to give my feedback on some aspects within the DFA and some that should be considered alongside those: - Regarding Dark Patterns within video games: I have lost a 15 year friendship because I wasn't able to recover an account they spent over a 1000 into through "gachapon" mechanics (spending money to purchase digital currencies, which are then used to gamble on a random occurence for digital products such as playable characters, skins and other game related digital items). A lot of those games tend to prey on "Time-Limited Events" in order to take advantage of their "Fear of Missing Out", and manipulate players into spending more than they should within those events. Things like Time Limited Gachas, Time-Limited Battle Passes, Time-Limited Collaborations, Time-Limited Spending Events and many more are potential issues piling up altogether. A lot of games love to also use notifications on their cash shop items that cannot get removed until you purchase something within the shop... only to make it reappear afterwards within a timeframe. These prey on both adults and children alike, and should be regulated. Those are but a tiny fraction of the potential dark patterns used, and combined with the addictive nature of video games (because video games are fun, and fun is addictive!), this is very dangerous and abused against minors and adults alike, especially children. - Regarding harmful advertising practices, I do believe that while influencers must be made responsible for knowingly endorsing harmful products, companies should also be included within the scope of this act. For example, there are companies that will put games on storefronts like the Android Play Store, and blatantly use gameplay from other games, fake gameplay elements in order to funnel them to aforementioned Dark Pattern laden stores. Unfortunately, those digital storefronts have an active incentive in letting those games stay, and as a matter of fact will promote them over any other game due to their highly lucrative and predatory nature. This should be considered in this act. - Something for personalization that should be treated within this act is the lack of preemptive protection against known bad actors within those digital storefronts, all the while those still benefit from those actors' harmful actions through things like sales or sponsorships. While it would be unrealistic to expect storefronts to always regulate and act on 3rd Parties' offers, because not only would the costs be impossible to keep up with, but some actions can also be "legal", yet "harmful" to users (scalping would be a good example of that). This act should consider requiring all digital storefronts hosting 3rd Party content to allow users to "blacklist" those 3rd Parties for their searches and their future served content in an easily doable manner (much like "blocking" someone on a social media erases their presence on your feed, or subscribing to a community made blacklist to prevent a group of bad actors from appearing), including in content related to sponsorships. This would allow for self-curation of harmful digital and physical products, as well as community curated lists of 3rd Parties to blacklist, deincentivizing all bad practices and helping consumers of digital storefronts, all the while helping those storefronts auto-regulate their products and services through the help of their own users. - Finally, deceptive pricing practices. Storefronts should be punished for deceptive or deceitful practices in regards to their prices. Constantly shifting the "base prices" in order to give deeper discounts, or selling certain items at a discounted price, only for them to not respect that price at checkout. Carrefour is an example of this. Thank you for reading.
